architecture.summary: The system applies sparse autoencoders (TopK SAEs) to FlatASCEND, a 14.5M-parameter autoregressive clinical sequence model trained on EHR data. SAEs decompose residual stream activations at all 10 transformer depth layers into interpretable sparse features. Information flows from EHR tokens through the frozen clinical model, whose activations are read by trained SAE encoders that project into a learned sparse dictionary space, then reconstructed; downstream analysis applies linear probes and intervention methods (delta-mode perturbation) to probe feature-level representations for clinical tasks.
trained_components: TopK sparse autoencoders (one per residual stream layer)
linear probes for mortality and length-of-stay prediction

biblio.title: Sparse Autoencoder Decomposition of Clinical Sequence Model Representations: Feature Complexity, Task Specialisation, and Mortality Prediction

claims.statement: SAE decomposition reveals progressive abstraction across transformer depth: layer-0 features are near-perfect token detectors while layer-6 features span approximately 30 token types across multiple clinical categories
Layer-0 features are near-perfect token detectors with 45.7% singleton features

SAE features outperform dense representations for discrete event prediction (mortality) under full-sequence simple linear probes, but dense representations outperform for continuous magnitude prediction (length of stay)
In clinically relevant leakage-safe windows, dense representations match or exceed SAE features across all tested settings: eICU-CRD 48-hour mortality AUC SAE 0.871 versus dense 0.880
MIMIC-IV mortality AUC: SAE 0.836 versus dense 0.914
INSPECT 1-year/3-year mortality AUC: SAE 0.697 versus dense 0.800

Feature reproducibility across random seeds is 21%, and individual features should be interpreted as illustrative rather than stable. Delta-mode intervention effects are larger than random controls in 3 of 4 conditions but not formally significant
